name: Hartle-Hawking no-boundary proposal
summary: The wave function of the universe is given by a path integral over compact, positive-definite Euclidean four-geometries that have the present three-geometry as their only boundary. Removes the need for a separately-specified initial condition by making the initial state computable from the Euclidean action of regular four-geometries. Predicts a specific (smooth, low-perturbation, inflationary) start of the universe.

addresses_puzzles.note: Provides a definite boundary condition replacing arbitrary pre-inflationary initial conditions. Predicts that a smooth, inflationary universe is preferred. Vilenkin's tunneling proposal is the main competitor; the two give different probability distributions (Hartle-Hawking is more dispersed in field-theory-space; Vilenkin is more concentrated).

introduces.note: Boundary-condition prescription on the wave function of the universe. No new fields or scales.

history: Hartle and Hawking (1983), 'Wave function of the universe', Phys. Rev. D 28, 2960. Foundational for quantum cosmology; competing with Vilenkin's tunneling-from-nothing proposal. Modern Lorentzian-path-integral analyses (Feldbrugge-Lehners-Turok 2017) have challenged the original WKB prescription, sparking active controversy.

references.citation: Hartle and Hawking, 'Wave function of the universe', Phys. Rev. D 28, 2960 (1983)
